questions.stem: An organelle is best described as:

options.text: A discrete subunit of a cell adapted to a specific function

questions.explanation: Organelles are discrete subunits of cells, each adapted to perform a specific function.

questions.stem: Which of these IS considered an organelle?
questions.explanation: Nuclei, vesicles, ribosomes and the plasma membrane are organelles; the cell wall, cytoskeleton and cytoplasm are not.

questions.stem: Which of these is NOT considered an organelle?
questions.explanation: Cytoplasm (along with the cell wall and cytoskeleton) is not classed as an organelle.

questions.scenario: The study of individual organelles became possible only after ultracentrifuges were developed.
questions.stem: This illustrates that scientific progress often follows:
questions.explanation: Progress in science often follows new techniques; cell fractionation by ultracentrifuge enabled the study of organelles.

questions.scenario: In eukaryotes, the nucleus and cytoplasm are separate compartments.
questions.stem: What advantage does this separation provide?
questions.explanation: Separating nucleus and cytoplasm separates transcription from translation, allowing mRNA processing before translation.

questions.stem: Because their nucleus and cytoplasm are separated, eukaryotes can carry out which process on mRNA before translation?

options.text: Immediate translation as it is made

questions.explanation: The separation allows post-transcriptional modification of mRNA before it reaches ribosomes in the cytoplasm.

questions.scenario: In prokaryotes, there is no nuclear membrane.
questions.stem: What is a consequence for gene expression?

options.text: mRNA may immediately meet ribosomes for translation

questions.explanation: Without compartmentalization, prokaryotic mRNA can be translated by ribosomes immediately as it is transcribed.

questions.stem: Which is an advantage of compartmentalization within the cytoplasm?

options.text: Metabolites and enzymes can be concentrated together

questions.explanation: Compartmentalization concentrates enzymes and metabolites and separates incompatible biochemical processes.

questions.scenario: Lysosomes keep digestive enzymes enclosed within a membrane.
questions.stem: Why is this an advantage?

options.text: It separates destructive enzymes from the rest of the cell
It stops the enzymes from working ever
It removes the need for a membrane
It makes the whole cytoplasm acidic

questions.explanation: Compartmentalizing digestive enzymes in lysosomes keeps incompatible, destructive processes separate from the cytoplasm.

questions.stem: Which structures are given as examples of compartmentalization in the cytoplasm?
questions.explanation: Lysosomes and phagocytic vacuoles illustrate the advantages of compartmentalization in the cytoplasm.

questions.stem: Why is the cell wall NOT classified as an organelle?

options.text: It is not a discrete internal subunit adapted to a specific cellular function in this classification
It is a type of vesicle

questions.explanation: By the syllabus definition, the cell wall, cytoskeleton and cytoplasm are not organelles.

questions.stem: The separation of transcription and translation is possible in eukaryotes because:

options.text: The nucleus and cytoplasm are separate compartments
Their DNA is naked and looped

questions.explanation: The nuclear membrane separates transcription (in the nucleus) from translation (in the cytoplasm).

questions.scenario: Two enzyme reactions in a cell would interfere with each other if mixed.
questions.stem: Compartmentalization helps by:
questions.explanation: Compartments allow incompatible biochemical processes to be separated within the cell.

questions.stem: Which list contains only structures classified as organelles?
questions.explanation: Nucleus, vesicles, ribosomes and the plasma membrane are organelles; cell wall, cytoplasm and cytoskeleton are not.

questions.stem: Concentrating enzymes and substrates within a compartment tends to:
questions.explanation: By concentrating enzymes and metabolites together, compartmentalization increases the efficiency of reactions.

questions.stem: Post-transcriptional modification of mRNA happens:

options.text: In the nucleus, before the mRNA reaches ribosomes

questions.explanation: Because the nucleus is separate, mRNA can be modified before it meets cytoplasmic ribosomes.

questions.scenario: A researcher uses cell fractionation to isolate organelles.
questions.stem: Which technology made this possible?
questions.explanation: Cell fractionation, enabled by the ultracentrifuge, allowed the study of individual organelles.

questions.stem: A phagocytic vacuole is an example of compartmentalization because it:

options.text: Encloses ingested material within a membrane
Is part of the cell wall

questions.explanation: Phagocytic vacuoles enclose ingested material, an example of the benefits of compartmentalization.

questions.stem: Which cell type can begin translating mRNA while it is still being transcribed?
questions.explanation: Without a nuclear membrane, prokaryotic mRNA can meet ribosomes and be translated immediately.

questions.stem: The plasma membrane is classified as an organelle, but the cytoskeleton is not. This shows that:

options.text: The classification of organelles is defined and specific
Every cell structure is an organelle

questions.explanation: The syllabus specifically classifies the plasma membrane, nuclei, vesicles and ribosomes as organelles, but not the cytoskeleton, cell wall or cytoplasm.

questions.stem: Overall, compartmentalization in the cytoplasm improves cell function by:

options.text: Organising reactions and separating incompatible ones

questions.explanation: Compartmentalization concentrates enzymes/metabolites and keeps incompatible processes apart, improving efficiency.

questions.stem: Which best summarises the benefit of a separate nucleus?

options.text: It allows transcription and mRNA processing to occur apart from translation
It lets DNA leave the cell
It removes the need for ribosomes

questions.explanation: A separate nucleus keeps transcription and mRNA modification apart from cytoplasmic translation.

questions.stem: Which feature do all organelles share by definition?

options.text: They are subunits adapted to specific functions
They are all found only in plants

questions.explanation: Organelles are discrete subunits adapted to specific functions; note ribosomes are organelles but not membrane-bound.

questions.stem: Which is a direct benefit of concentrating specific enzymes in one compartment?

options.text: Reactions requiring those enzymes proceed more efficiently

questions.explanation: Concentrating enzymes with their substrates raises reaction efficiency, a benefit of compartmentalization.

questions.stem: Which is a key difference between prokaryotes and eukaryotes in gene expression?

options.text: Only eukaryotes separate transcription and translation into compartments

questions.explanation: Eukaryotes separate transcription and translation by compartments, whereas prokaryotes do not.

questions.stem: Which adaptation of the mitochondrion increases the surface area for aerobic respiration?

options.text: Folded cristae of the inner membrane

questions.explanation: The large surface area of cristae is a mitochondrial adaptation for ATP production by aerobic respiration.

questions.stem: The enzymes and substrates of the Krebs cycle are compartmentalized in the mitochondrial:
questions.explanation: The Krebs cycle enzymes and substrates are compartmentalized in the matrix of the mitochondrion.

questions.stem: The mitochondrion has a small intermembrane space because it has:
questions.explanation: A double membrane with a small intermembrane space is an adaptation supporting ATP production.

questions.stem: Which chloroplast structure provides a large surface area holding photosystems?
questions.explanation: The large surface area of thylakoid membranes carries the photosystems for the light-dependent reactions.

questions.stem: The enzymes and substrates of the Calvin cycle are compartmentalized in the chloroplast's:
questions.explanation: The Calvin cycle enzymes and substrates are compartmentalized in the stroma of the chloroplast.

questions.stem: Small volumes of fluid inside thylakoids are an adaptation that helps:

options.text: Build up concentrations for the light-dependent reactions

questions.explanation: The small thylakoid volume allows concentrations to build up, supporting efficient photosynthesis.

questions.stem: Pores in the nuclear membrane are needed for:

options.text: Exchange of materials between nucleus and cytoplasm

questions.explanation: Nuclear pores allow controlled exchange, such as mRNA leaving the nucleus, across the double membrane.

questions.scenario: During mitosis and meiosis, the nuclear membrane must:
questions.stem: What happens to the nuclear membrane?
questions.explanation: The nuclear membrane breaks into vesicles during mitosis and meiosis, a functional benefit of its structure.

questions.stem: Free ribosomes in the cytoplasm mainly synthesize proteins that are:

options.text: Retained for use within the cell

questions.explanation: Free ribosomes make proteins for use inside the cell; membrane-bound ribosomes make proteins for transport or secretion.

questions.stem: Ribosomes on the rough endoplasmic reticulum synthesize proteins mainly for:

options.text: Transport within the cell and secretion

questions.explanation: The rough ER's membrane-bound ribosomes make proteins destined for transport within the cell and for secretion.

questions.stem: The Golgi apparatus is chiefly involved in:
questions.explanation: The Golgi apparatus processes and secretes proteins.

questions.stem: Which protein is involved in the formation of vesicles?
questions.explanation: Clathrin plays a role in the formation of vesicles.

questions.scenario: A cell that uses a lot of energy has many mitochondria with extensive cristae.
questions.stem: How does this benefit the cell?

options.text: More surface area increases ATP production

questions.explanation: Extensive cristae provide more surface area for the reactions of aerobic respiration, boosting ATP output.

questions.stem: Which pair correctly matches organelle region to process?

options.text: Chloroplast stroma — Calvin cycle; mitochondrial matrix — Krebs cycle
Chloroplast stroma — Krebs cycle; mitochondrial matrix — Calvin cycle
Both cycles occur in the thylakoid
Both cycles occur in the cristae

questions.explanation: The Calvin cycle is in the chloroplast stroma; the Krebs cycle is in the mitochondrial matrix.

questions.scenario: A cell secretes large amounts of protein.
questions.stem: Which organelle would you expect to be well developed?
questions.explanation: The rough ER, with membrane-bound ribosomes, is extensive in cells that make proteins for secretion.

questions.stem: A functional benefit of the nucleus having a double membrane is that it:

options.text: Can break into vesicles during cell division and has pores for exchange
Prevents all exchange with the cytoplasm

questions.explanation: The double membrane has pores for exchange and can break into vesicles during mitosis and meiosis.

questions.scenario: A protein made on the rough ER needs to be secreted.
questions.stem: Which organelle processes and packages it for secretion?
questions.explanation: The Golgi apparatus processes proteins and packages them, often into secretory vesicles.

questions.stem: Vesicles in cells function mainly to:

options.text: Transport materials within or out of the cell

questions.explanation: Vesicles transport materials within the cell and to the membrane; clathrin helps in their formation.

questions.stem: Which set of mitochondrial adaptations supports aerobic respiration?

options.text: Double membrane, cristae, enzymes compartmentalized in the matrix
Single membrane and no internal folds
A cellulose wall and central vacuole

questions.explanation: Mitochondria have a double membrane, folded cristae and Krebs-cycle enzymes in the matrix, all aiding ATP production.

questions.stem: The key contrast between free and membrane-bound ribosomes is the:

options.text: Destination of the proteins they make
Type of nucleic acid they contain

questions.explanation: Free ribosomes make proteins retained in the cell; rough-ER ribosomes make proteins for transport and secretion.

questions.scenario: A cell carries out a great deal of photosynthesis.
questions.stem: Which organelle adaptation would you expect to be prominent?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts adapted for photosynthesis have extensive thylakoid membranes carrying photosystems.

questions.stem: Nuclear pores are essential because they allow:

options.text: mRNA to move out to the cytoplasm

questions.explanation: Pores in the nuclear membrane let molecules such as mRNA pass between nucleus and cytoplasm.

questions.stem: The Golgi apparatus can be thought of as the cell's:
questions.explanation: The Golgi processes and secretes proteins, acting as a processing and dispatch centre.

questions.stem: Clathrin's role in vesicle formation involves:

options.text: Helping the membrane curve and pinch off a vesicle

questions.explanation: Clathrin coats and shapes the membrane during vesicle formation.

questions.stem: Compartmentalizing the Krebs cycle enzymes in the matrix is advantageous because it:

options.text: Concentrates enzymes and substrates for efficient respiration

questions.explanation: Keeping the Krebs cycle enzymes and substrates together in the matrix enables efficient aerobic respiration.
